diary: Woke up in Redmond before the sun really got going, did my careful little routine and checked the tide/weather notes like I always do—Puget Sound doesn’t care if I’m sleepy. By the time I headed toward Kirkland, everything looked bright and easy, the kind of June morning that makes you believe the day will cooperate. I parked near work, got my instruments laid out, and spent the late morning nerding through data and prepping containers so they’d actually stay sealed. Lunch at Whole Foods (Totem Lake) felt pleasantly calm, and I grabbed a few supplies at REI without getting caught in any chaos. The FIFA match airing at 11am was definitely in the air, so I kept things low-key—early dinner instead of chasing crowds—then met my friend at The Georgian on Capitol Hill. We talked, walked a bit, and watched the sky at Volunteer Park. Stargazing quietly felt like a reward, and I went to bed feeling steady and lucky.
thought_process: I tried to design the day around both the science and the city’s rhythm. With the light rail closed between Westlake and University District, I stuck to driving and kept buffers for errands, so I wouldn’t arrive flustered or under-caffeinated. The sunny weather made me extra careful about hydration and timing, because warm afternoons can mess with how I handle samples and how my focus holds. Knowing the USA vs Wales match would pull people downtown, I intentionally leaned into quiet meals and a calmer meetup at The Georgian, rather than getting tempted into packed bars. My mood shifted gradually—calm competence in the morning, light social warmth in the evening, then a calmer, almost reverent feeling while stargazing. World events didn’t derail me; they just nudged where I chose to be.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
